definition: Systems that autonomously plan and carry out multi-step tasks toward a goal with little human prompting.
A software entity that acts on a user's behalf to complete a task, often calling tools or data to do it.
Applying machine learning to automate analysis, surface insights, and generate predictions or recommendations from data.
The policies and controls that keep artificial intelligence use safe, compliant, and accountable across an organization.
When a generative model produces confident output that is factually wrong or unsupported by its sources.
Coordinating multiple AI models, tools, and data sources so they work together in one workflow.
Data that has been cleaned, governed, and structured so AI and machine learning models can trust and use it.
A subscription model where BI and analysis run on a vendor-managed cloud platform instead of on-premise hardware.
An interactive interface for exploring, tracking, and drilling into KPIs and the metrics behind them.
An open-source distributed event-streaming platform built to ingest and transform real-time data feeds at scale.
Using AI and machine learning inside a BI platform to enhance human intuition across the full analysis lifecycle.
Tools that automate building, training, and deploying machine learning models so non-experts can produce them.
A business-intelligence surface that tracks and reports KPIs and metrics drawn from enterprise reporting sources.
Collecting, preparing, and analyzing very large and varied datasets to surface insights ordinary tools can't handle.
An interactive visualization tool for monitoring and examining an organization's operational KPIs and metrics.
The applications, processes, and infrastructure that turn raw information into analysis for better decisions and performance.
Using a BI tool to prepare and analyze data, then package and share actionable findings with stakeholders.
Software applications used to collect, combine, and analyze business-relevant information for decision-making.
Recording changes in a Microsoft SQL Server database and delivering those changes to a downstream system.
Identifying changes made to data in a database and streaming them in real time to downstream systems.
A business user who builds models and advanced analyses with low-code tools, without formal analytics training.
Running data analysis and BI on a public or private cloud rather than on a company's own on-premise servers.
A managed, scalable warehouse delivered as a service in a public cloud and tuned for BI and analytics.
Querying data and getting insights through a chat-style, plain-language interface instead of dashboards.
A consolidated screen of critical data, visualizations, and KPIs tailored to a user segment for fast review.
Letting users visualize KPIs and interact with the underlying data directly on the display to analyze trends.
Applications that build visual representations of data and KPIs so users can spot patterns and decide faster.
Combining datasets from multiple sources into one format and summarizing them to support analysis.
Using processes and technology to combine and examine datasets, find patterns, and extract valuable insights.
Software that helps users uncover patterns, trends, and relationships hidden in their data.
An organized inventory of an organization's data assets, indexed by metadata for on-demand, business-ready access.
An interactive tool for tracking, analyzing, and displaying KPIs and metrics from across the business.
Making data accessible to non-technical people across an organization so anyone can use it to decide.
Exploring and analyzing data to uncover patterns and relationships that improve decisions and performance.
Reviewing a raw dataset to learn its characteristics and spot initial patterns before deeper analysis.
A machine-driven integration architecture that uses metadata to unify and govern scattered data environments.
The roles, policies, and tools that keep data quality high and usage proper across the whole data lifecycle.
Collecting data from various sources and moving it to a target site, in batches or in real time.
Synchronizing data across applications and platforms to give users one accurate, up-to-date view for analytics.
The accuracy, consistency, and completeness of data maintained throughout its entire lifecycle.
A central repository that stores raw structured and unstructured data at any scale with no need to structure it first.
A design that keeps compute and storage inside one platform so raw data can be accessed and analyzed rapidly.
An architecture that blends a warehouse's structured querying and governance with a lake's cheap raw storage.
Tracing and visualizing how data flows from source to destination and what transforms it along the way.
The ability to read, analyze, and communicate with data and ask the right questions to drive decisions.
Collecting, storing, organizing, and maintaining data so it can support analysis and decision-making.
A purpose-built repository serving the analytical needs of a single department, business line, or region.
An architecture that decentralizes ownership, letting the teams who use data manage it on a self-serve platform.
Moving data between storage systems, applications, or formats, usually as a one-time prep-and-transfer effort.
Using statistics and machine learning to discover hidden patterns, correlations, and anomalies in large datasets.
Creating a diagram that defines the structure, attributes, and relationships of a system's data entities.
Continuously monitoring the health, freshness, and reliability of data across pipelines to catch issues early.
An automated set of tools and processes that move and transform data from a source system to a target.
Trusted, reusable, consumable data assets designed deliberately for specific domain business outcomes.
How well a dataset meets standards for accuracy, consistency, reliability, completeness, and timeliness.
Creating and maintaining identical copies of data across multiple locations, in real time or on a schedule.
An interdisciplinary field combining statistics, programming, and domain expertise to extract insight from data.
The hands-on role of managing and safeguarding specific data assets to keep them accurate and properly used.
A structured plan for how an organization will manage, use, and extract value from its data assets.
Cleaning, validating, and reshaping data so it matches the structure a target system requires.
The emerging directions and shifts shaping how organizations use BI and data, and how to exploit them.
A flexible, scalable modeling approach for warehousing that handles complex structures and enterprise analytics.
Presenting information as graphs, charts, and maps so people can quickly uncover actionable insights.
Software that turns large volumes of data into graphics and imagery to support more informed decisions.
A management system that aggregates large volumes from many sources into one structured, historical repository.
Automating the full warehouse lifecycle, from modeling and ingestion to marts and governance, to speed analytics.
Cleaning, structuring, and transforming raw data into a usable format ready for analysis.
Copying data from a primary database to one or more replicas to boost availability, fault tolerance, and reliability.
A methodology that improves the communication, integration, and automation of data flows between producers and consumers.
Analytics software that gathers and analyzes data to guide business decision-making.
A subset of machine learning that uses multi-layered neural networks to model complex patterns.
An open-source storage layer that adds database-style ACID reliability and performance to a data lake.
Summarizing and interpreting historical data to understand past events, patterns, and trends.
An electronic interface for tracking, analyzing, and reporting on KPIs and metrics.
An integration sequence that extracts data, loads it raw into the target, then transforms it there using the target's compute.
Building data analysis and reporting capabilities directly into another software application's interface.
Numeric representations of text or images that capture meaning so machines can compare them by similarity.
An integration sequence that extracts data, transforms it, then loads it into a target repository.
The connected set of processes that extract, transform, and load data from a source into a target repository.
Software that consolidates multi-sourced data into a common format and loads it into a warehouse or mart.
Techniques that reveal the reasoning behind a machine learning model's output so people can trust it.
Examining financial statements and related data to judge an organization's health and performance.
Combining and analyzing datasets to gain insight into an organization's financial performance.
A large model pre-trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks.
Models that create new text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from large training datasets.
A visual representation of data that users can explore and analyze directly within the graphic itself.
A cloud platform that integrates applications and data sources across cloud and on-premise environments.
An API that processes real-time feeds from source systems into message sequences apps and analytics can consume.
A network of entities and their relationships that lets machines connect and reason over linked facts.
A quantifiable measure of performance over time tracked against a specific objective.
A display of key indicators in interactive charts and graphs for quick, organized performance review.
A graphical, real-time view of key metrics that helps leaders track company, department, or project performance.
A neural network trained on massive text corpora to understand and produce human-readable content.
A field of AI where systems learn patterns from data to improve at a task without being explicitly programmed.
Combining and analyzing datasets to find patterns that improve the ROI of marketing efforts.
Quantifiable performance measures tied to specific strategic marketing objectives.
Maintaining one consistent, authoritative version of core business entities like customers and products.
Organizing and controlling the descriptive data that defines the technical, business, or operational traits of other data.
Practices for deploying, monitoring, and maintaining machine learning models reliably in production.
An open standard that lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources in a consistent way.
Models that take in and combine multiple input types, such as text, images, and audio, at once.
The branch of AI that enables computers to interpret and work with human speech and writing.
Asking a data system questions in plain everyday words instead of writing code or formal syntax.
Technology that organizes data into multidimensional cubes for fast slicing, dicing, and aggregation in analysis.
Analyzing workforce data to improve hiring, productivity, engagement, and talent retention.
Using statistical modeling and machine learning on past and current data to forecast future outcomes.
A statistical technique that uses historical data to predict the outcome of future events.
Advanced analysis that recommends the optimal action or strategy to take next, not just what will happen.
Crafting and refining the instructions given to a generative model to steer accurate, useful output.
Analyzing and responding to information about customers, products, or apps the moment it's generated.
Information made available for use as soon as it is generated, with no storage delay.
The practice of designing and using AI ethically, with fairness, transparency, and accountability built in.
Grounding a model's answers in external data fetched at query time to cut down on fabrication.
A model that aligns customer-facing teams behind shared goals to accelerate revenue.
Pushing transformed data from a warehouse back into operational tools like CRMs so teams can act on it.
An approach that lets business users explore data and build their own reports without relying on IT.
A business-friendly abstraction that maps raw data into consistent, reusable metrics and definitions for users.
One trusted, central reference for a given data point that every team relies on instead of conflicting copies.
Collecting and manipulating location data like addresses, GPS, and satellite imagery to uncover place-based insights.
Data that flows continuously from source to target, usually generated at high speed by many sources at once.
Combining data across systems to gain insight into procuring, processing, and distributing goods.
Artificially generated data that mimics real datasets, used to train models without exposing sensitive records.
A store optimized to index and search high-dimensional embeddings, the engine behind AI similarity search.
Pairing computational analysis with interactive visuals so users can explore and manipulate data in new ways.
